title: Dyeing with madder for red
subtitle: Getting a true red from Rubia tinctorum root on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Madder (Rubia tinctorum) is the primary red dye plant of European and Near Eastern traditions, used for millennia to dye wool, cotton, and silk. The colour shifts considerably with temperature and pH: high heat produces brick-orange; controlled temperature and neutral pH produce a clear, warm red.
sourceNotes: Madder dyeing is documented extensively in natural dyeing texts including Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour' and in the historical textile record. The temperature control requirement (not above 82°C) is consistent across sources. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: The primary red pigment compound in madder root (Rubia tinctorum). Alizarin bonds to alum-mordanted wool to produce a warm red. At temperatures above 85°C, alizarin converts to less stable compounds and the colour shifts to dull orange-brown.
A substance added to a mordant or dye bath to shift the final colour. Chalk (calcium carbonate) added to a madder bath brightens the red; iron (iron sulphate) saddens and darkens it toward burgundy.

Extracting dye from plant material by soaking in cold water overnight rather than heating. For madder, a cold overnight soak extracts more pure alizarin and less of the orange-brown purpurin pigments that degrade colour clarity.

content.text: and a range of related pigments. Keeping the dye bath below 82°C preserves alizarin and produces a warm red. Boiling converts alizarin to orange-brown purpurin and other less stable pigments, producing a dull brick colour. Temperature control is the primary skill in madder dyeing. Use a
the night before dyeing to improve colour clarity.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Wear dedicated gloves, work in a ventilated space, and keep mordant jars away from food and children.

items.name: Alum-mordanted wool yarn or fleece, pre-wetted
Dried madder root, ground or chopped
items.qty: 50-100 g (50-100% WOF for medium to deep red)

items.name: Stainless steel dye pot, minimum 4 litre
Chalk or bicarbonate of soda (optional, to shift toward red)

content.text: The evening before dyeing, place the madder root in the dye pot and cover with cold water. Leave to soak overnight. This cold extraction draws out alizarin in preference to the orange-brown purpurin that is released at higher temperatures. Do not heat at this stage.
Step 2: Heat the dye bath to 60°C and enter the fibre

The next day, heat the madder soak water and root together to 60°C. Enter the pre-wetted, mordanted fibre. Raise the temperature slowly to 78-80°C over 40 minutes, watching the thermometer closely. Do not let the bath exceed 82°C. Maintain 78-80°C for 60 minutes, stirring gently. This is the maximum temperature range for a true red from madder.

If the colour looks orange or brick-toned rather than red, the bath pH may be too acidic. A small pinch of chalk (calcium carbonate) added to the bath and stirred in will shift the pH slightly toward neutral and brighten the red. Check with pH strips: a slightly alkaline or neutral bath (pH 7-7.5) produces the clearest red. Do not use too much chalk or the bath will foam.

Allow the bath to cool to 40°C. Lift the fibre from the bath (the root can be strained out separately). Rinse in warm water until clear. Dry in shade. Madder-dyed wool lightens slightly on drying but should hold a warm terracotta-to-coral-red. A
iron afterbath (a solution of iron sulphate applied after dyeing) shifts the red to burgundy-brown.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to madder dyeing.

items.symptom: Colour is brick-orange or brown rather than red
items.cause: The dye bath exceeded 82°C at some point during the session, converting alizarin to purpurin.
items.fix: Monitor the thermometer continuously. If the bath is already above temperature, remove the fibre, cool the bath, then re-enter. The shift to orange is partially reversible if caught early.

items.symptom: Colour washes out significantly in the first wash
items.cause: Surface dye that was not fully bonded: usually means the mordanting was insufficient or the dyeing temperature was too low for too little time.
items.fix: Check mordant percentage (should be 10-15% WOF alum). Re-dye in a fresh madder bath at correct temperature.
